Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent General Surgery digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 13 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in General Surgery.

13articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Annals of surgery

Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery vs Glucagon-like peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Therapy: A Head-to-Head Comparison in Improvement of Cardiometabolic Risk Profiles

Ghusn W, Vierkant RA, Jawhar N et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: How do metabolic and bariatric surgery and GLP-1 receptor agonists compare for reducing cardiovascular risk in adults with obesity?
Key Findings:
  • Both treatments reduced 10-year ASCVD risk similarly (-0.8% vs -1.1%, p=0.36), but bariatric surgery achieved significantly greater lifetime ASCVD risk reduction (-8.6% vs -1.7%, p<0.001)
  • Bariatric surgery produced superior weight loss (-27.8% vs -11.1%, p<0.001) and more favourable lipid profile changes
  • After adjustment, bariatric surgery remained independently associated with greater lifetime ASCVD risk reduction (β -6.92, 95% CI -9.22 to -4.62)
Clinical Relevance: This evidence supports bariatric surgery as the more effective long-term cardiovascular risk reduction strategy for obese patients, informing MDT discussions and referral decisions within NHS obesity services.
Limitations: Retrospective design with potential selection bias, as GLP-1RA patients were older with higher baseline cardiovascular risk.
PubMed →DOI →
Annals of surgery

Mucosal Impedance in the Diagnosis and Surgical Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

Eriksson SE, Rappaport J, Ayazi S · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: How can mucosal impedance measurement improve diagnosis and surgical management of gastroesophageal reflux disease?
Key Findings:
  • Mucosal impedance directly measures esophageal mucosal integrity at tissue level, showing reduced values in GERD patients including non-erosive disease
  • MI values improve following effective medical or surgical therapy, potentially providing objective treatment response assessment
  • MI may complement conventional pH studies by assessing cumulative reflux injury rather than just acid exposure time
Clinical Relevance: This technique could enhance patient selection for anti-reflux surgery and provide objective outcome measures beyond symptom scores, particularly relevant given NHS emphasis on evidence-based surgical referrals.
Limitations: As a narrative review, it does not provide systematic analysis of the evidence quality or comparative effectiveness data needed for clinical implementation.
PubMed →DOI →
Annals of surgery

Long-term Outcomes of Fenestrated and Branched Endovascular Aortic Repair for Complex Aortic Aneurysms in A high-volume Aortic Center

Nana P, Apostolidis G, Panuccio G et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What are the long-term outcomes of fenestrated and branched endovascular aortic repair (f/bEVAR) for complex aortic aneurysms in a high-volume centre?
Key Findings:
  • Technical success achieved in 93.6% of 916 patients, with 30-day mortality of 7.4% overall (4.4% elective cases)
  • Spinal cord ischemia occurred in 16% of patients (12.7% elective), with 2.9% experiencing permanent paraplegia
  • 60-month survival was 82.6%, but freedom from aortic reintervention was only 40.0%
Clinical Relevance: This large series demonstrates that f/bEVAR achieves acceptable mortality and paraplegia rates for complex aneurysms, though frequent reinterventions are required—relevant for UK centres developing complex aortic programmes.
Limitations: Single-centre retrospective study spanning 14 years with evolving techniques and devices may limit generalisability to other centres.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA surgery

Early Enteral vs Oral Postoperative Nutrition After Pancreatoduodenectomy: The NUTRIWHI Randomized Clinical Trial

Joliat GR, Martin D, Labgaa I et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Does early enteral nutrition via nasojejunal tube reduce postoperative complications compared to oral nutrition alone in nutritionally at-risk patients after pancreatoduodenectomy?
Key Findings:
  • Early enteral nutrition significantly reduced mean 90-day comprehensive complication index compared to oral nutrition (25.5 vs 35.8, mean difference 10.3, 95% CI 1.8-18.8, p=0.02)
  • Overall morbidity rates at 90 days were similar between groups (76% vs 86%, p=0.18)
  • No differences in specific complications including delayed gastric emptying, pancreatic fistula, or surgical site infections
Clinical Relevance: This supports implementing early enteral nutrition protocols for nutritionally at-risk pancreatoduodenectomy patients in UK hepatobiliary units, potentially reducing overall complication burden while maintaining similar major complication rates.
Limitations: High dropout rate (17%) and nasojejunal tube replacement required in 24% of enteral nutrition patients due to inadvertent removal.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA surgery

Prevention of Parastomal Hernia With Funnel-Shaped Intra-Abdominal Mesh vs No Mesh for Rectal Adenocarcinoma Surgery: 3-Year Follow-Up of a Randomized Clinical Trial

Mäkäräinen E, Järvinen J, Kössi J et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Does prophylactic funnel-shaped mesh placement reduce parastomal hernia incidence following permanent colostomy for rectal adenocarcinoma?
Key Findings:
  • CT-confirmed parastomal hernia at 3 years: 57% with mesh vs 82% without mesh (absolute risk reduction 25%, 95% CI 5-42%, p=0.001)
  • Clinically diagnosed parastomal hernia: 10% with mesh vs 39% without mesh (absolute risk reduction 29%, 95% CI 13-44%, p<0.001)
  • Hernias in the mesh group were significantly smaller (median volume 87ml vs 186ml, p=0.003)
Clinical Relevance: This supports routine prophylactic mesh use during permanent colostomy formation in UK colorectal practice, potentially reducing the substantial burden of parastomal hernia repairs on NHS surgical services.
Limitations: High attrition rate with only 71% of randomized patients available for final CT analysis at 3 years.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA surgery

Air Change Setbacks and Surgical Outcomes and Energy Use in Operating Rooms

Alipouriani A, Bennett WC, Deverakonda DL et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Insufficient information provided.
Key Question: The abstract content is not provided, making it impossible to determine what clinical question this study addresses regarding air change setbacks, surgical outcomes, and energy use in operating rooms.
Key Findings: Cannot be determined without access to the abstract content.
Clinical Relevance: Cannot be assessed without the study details, though the title suggests this may relate to optimising operating theatre ventilation systems, which could be relevant for NHS infection control and sustainability initiatives.
Limitations: Cannot evaluate limitations without access to the abstract content. Note: Only the title, authors, journal, and publication date were provided. The abstract content is required to produce a meaningful clinical summary of the study's methodology, findings, and implications for surgical practice.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the American College of Surgeons

Toolkit to Promote Collaboration Between Surgeons and Anesthesiologists: Addressing Common Barriers to Perioperative Teamwork

Wei HS, Wu F, Berry RE et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Qualitative phenomenological study
Key Question: What are the barriers to surgeon-anesthesiologist collaboration and what practical strategies can improve perioperative teamwork?
Key Findings:
  • Seven barrier themes identified including systems/professional identity issues, communication failures, relationship problems, and personality conflicts among 40 physicians interviewed
  • Five collaboration-enhancing strategies emerged: structured communication across all operative phases, relationship building, team-building attitudes/actions, and trust rebuilding
  • Practical tactics included fostering familiarity through introductions/first-name use, early case discussions with clinical reasoning explanations, and adopting shared mentality of openness and positive intent
Clinical Relevance: This research provides evidence-based strategies that UK general surgery departments can implement to improve surgeon-anesthesiologist relationships, potentially enhancing perioperative safety and efficiency within NHS theatre environments.
Limitations: US-based study may not fully translate to NHS context given different healthcare systems and professional hierarchies.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the American College of Surgeons

Surgeon Perspectives on Responsibility for Acute Lower Gastrointestinal Surgical Conditions: A Modified Delphi Study

Myers S, Murillo A, Rudel RK et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Modified Delphi study
Key Question: How should acute lower gastrointestinal surgical conditions be allocated between acute care surgeons and colorectal surgeons in teaching hospitals?
Key Findings:
  • Consensus achieved for only 10 of 20 conditions (50%) after first round, with remaining cases requiring contextual decision-making
  • Three key determinants identified: practice model differences (capacity for longitudinal care), specialty-specific training priorities, and patient clinical status
  • Hemodynamically unstable patients prioritised surgeon availability over subspecialty expertise
Clinical Relevance: This framework could inform service organisation and on-call arrangements in UK hospitals where both general surgeons and colorectal specialists provide emergency cover, potentially improving patient allocation and reducing conflicts over case ownership.
Limitations: Small expert panel (n=16) may not represent broader surgical practice patterns or different healthcare systems.
PubMed →DOI →
